items.title: Smoking as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Smoking

  • Smoking is the most relevant environmental risk factor, with a relative risk of 2-3 vs. non-smokers.
  • The risk persists up to 10-15 years after cessation; dose-dependent (no. pack-years).
  • Mechanism: smoke carcinogens (N-nitrosamines) induce KRAS mutation and damage in CDKN2A.
  • Synergy with type 2 diabetes mellitus and obesity, increasing the risk up to 5-6 times.
  • In ENARM/MIR: they ask that smoking explains up to 25-30% of cases.

items.title: KRAS mutation
items.contentHtml:
KRAS mutation

  • Present in >90% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas; point mutation in codon 12 (most common G12D).
  • It leads to constitutive activation of the MAPK pathway, independent of EGFR stimulation.
  • It is associated with resistance to anti-EGFR therapies (cetuximab, panitumumab); absolute contraindication in KRAS mutated tumors.
  • Detection in biopsy or cystic fluid (CPAM) is a diagnostic criterion for high risk of malignancy.
  • Under examination: differentiate from BRAF mutation (rare in pancreas, <5%), which also activates MAPK.

items.title: CA 19-9
items.contentHtml:
CA 19-9

  • CA 19-9 (sialyl-Lewis a) is the serum marker of choice in pancreatic adenocarcinoma, sensitivity 70-90% and specificity 80-90%.
  • It is not diagnostic on its own; it is elevated in cholestasis, pancreatitis and biliary tumors; false positive if obstructive jaundice.
  • Clinical threshold: >37 U/mL suggests malignancy; levels >1000 U/mL indicate unresectable or metastatic disease.
  • Useful for monitoring response to chemotherapy and detecting post-Whipple recurrence (elevation >2 times the nadir).
  • Patients with Lewis a-/b- phenotype (7% of the population) do not express CA 19-9 → false negatives.

items.title: obstructive jaundice
items.contentHtml:
Obstructive jaundice in the pancreatic head

  • Pancreatic head tumors compress the distal common bile duct, causing early obstructive jaundice (vs. body/tail that begins with pain and weight loss).
  • Laboratory: total bilirubin >3 mg/dL, elevated alkaline phosphatase, elevated GGT, pattern cholestatic.
  • Initial ultrasound: intra- and extrahepatic bile duct dilation (double duct sign: dilation of common bile duct and pancreatic duct).
  • MR cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) or helical computed tomography confirm level of obstruction and tumor extension.
  • endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography with preoperative biliary stent indicated only if bilirubin >15 mg/dL, cholangitis or surgical delay >2 weeks.

items.title: classical triad
items.contentHtml:
Triad: pain, weight loss, jaundice

  • The classic triad of abdominal pain (epigastric, radiating to the back), unexplained weight loss and jaundice appears in 70% of patients at diagnosis.
  • Pain: typically dull, continuous, worse with recumbency and improves with leaning forward (celiac plexus infiltration).
  • Weight loss: multifactorial (anorexia, malabsorption due to exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, tumor cachexia).
  • Jaundice: painless, progressive, with choluria and hypocolia, without fever (vs. cholangitis).
  • On examination: the presence of the complete triad suggests locally advanced or metastatic disease (stage III/intravenous).

items.title: Courvoisier-Terrier sign
items.contentHtml:
Courvoisier-Terrier sign

  • Definition: palpable, distended and non-painful gallbladder in a patient with obstructive jaundice, suggestive of pancreatic neoplasia.
  • Pathophysiology: chronic distal obstruction of the common bile duct due to pancreatic head tumor → the gallbladder is distended by retained bile without inflammation.
  • Contrast with choledocholithiasis: the gallbladder is usually contracted or inflamed (painful) due to acute obstruction.
  • Low sensitivity (<30%) but specificity >90% for malignancy; classic sign in surgical semiology.
  • In ENARM/EUNACOM: they ask about association with pancreatic cancer vs. Distal cholangiocarcinoma or ampulloma.

items.title: Klatskin tumor
items.contentHtml:
Klatskin tumor (negative Courvoisier sign).
  • magnetic resonance imaging with cholangiography and percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (CTP) are key for staging and planning resection.
  • Marker: CA 19-9 can be elevated, but so can CEA and CA 125.
  • Surgical resection (hepatectomy + lymphadenectomy) is the only curative treatment; prognosis slightly better than pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

items.title: computed tomography staging
items.contentHtml:
Staging with helical computed tomography

  • Triphasic helical computed tomography (arterial, pancreatic and portal phase) is the standard for local and distant staging.
  • Unresectability criteria: invasion of the superior mesenteric artery (>180°), celiac trunk, common hepatic artery, or distant metastasis.
  • Venous involvement (superior mesenteric/portal vein) is not an absolute contraindication if it can be reconstructed.
  • Sensitivity to detect liver metastases >90%; pathological lymph nodes if >10 mm or necrosis.
  • In ENARM: they ask that triphasic helical computed tomography is the first study of choice for staging (not magnetic resonance imaging or positron emission tomography).

items.title: Whipple
items.contentHtml:
Surgical resection (Whipple)

  • Whipple surgery (pancreatoduodenectomy) is the curative treatment for resectable pancreatic head tumors (stage I-II).
  • It includes en bloc resection of the pancreatic head, duodenum, gallbladder, distal common bile duct and, depending on the technique, gastric antrum (classic Whipple) or preservation of pylorus (traverse).
  • Resectability criteria: absence of arterial invasion (<180°), hepatic or peritoneal metastases, and good functional status.
  • Perioperative mortality <5% in high-volume centers; morbidity 30-50% (pancreatic fistula, delayed gastric emptying).
  • 5-year post-Whipple survival with adjuvant treatment: 20-25% (vs. <10% overall).

items.title: Chemotherapy
items.contentHtml:
Chemotherapy: FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine

  • In metastatic disease, FOLFIRINOX (5-FU, leucovorin, irinotecan, oxaliplatin) is first line in patients with ECOG 0-1, median survival 11.1 months vs. 6.8 months with gemcitabine.
  • Gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel is alternative in patients with ECOG 0-2 (median 8.5 months).
  • In adjuvant treatment, modified FOLFIRINOX (without 5-FU bolus) is standard after Whipple (PRODIGE 24 study).
  • In unresectable locally advanced disease, induction chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX) followed by evaluation for radiotherapy or surgical rescue.
  • In ENARM: they ask that FOLFIRINOX is superior to gemcitabine in response and survival, but with greater toxicity (neutropenia, diarrhea).

items.title: Liver metastases
items.contentHtml:
Hepatic metastases

  • The liver is the most common site of hematogenous metastasis (70-80% of patients with metastatic disease).
  • Portal pathway from the pancreas → the tumor drains directly to the portal venous system.
  • On helical computed tomography portal phase they appear as hypodense lesions, often multiple and bilobar.
  • The The presence of liver metastases classifies as stage IV (unresectable) and contraindicates curative surgery.
  • Liver biopsy guided by ultrasound or computed tomography confirms the diagnosis if there is doubt; Immunohistochemistry: CK7+/CK20- (similar to primary).

items.title: Forecast
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis

  • The 5-year overall survival is <10% (less than 5% if all stages are included).
  • Poor prognostic factors: advanced stage at diagnosis (>80% unresectable), CA 19-9 >1000 U/mL, positive lymph nodes, R1/R2 margins, poor differentiation.
  • In patients resected with negative margins (R0) and negative nodes, 5-year survival reaches 30-40%.
  • The median survival in metastatic disease with chemotherapy is 8-12 months; without treatment, <6 months.
  • In ENARM: they ask that the prognosis of pancreatic adenocarcinoma is the worst among gastrointestinal tumors.
